name: ssl
context: Configure SSL/TLS certificates, HTTPS termination, and secure cipher suites on nginx
info: This skill covers enabling HTTPS on nginx: installing SSL/TLS certificates, configuring secure protocol versions and cipher suites, setting up OCSP stapling, managing session caching for performance, automating certificate provisioning with certbot/Let's Encrypt, and implementing HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects. Nginx handles SSL termination at the proxy layer so backend servers don't need to manage certificates. The key directives are `ssl_certificate`, `ssl_certificate_key`, `ssl_protocols`, and `ssl_ciphers` inside a `server` block with `listen 443 ssl`. Agents use this skill to secure any site with HTTPS — from self-signed certs in development to production Let's Encrypt automation. See [[nginx/config]] for server block structure and [[nginx/reverse-proxy]] for proxying HTTPS-terminated traffic to backends.

sections.title: Certificate setup
sections.body: SSL requires two files: `ssl_certificate` (the server certificate + intermediate chain, concatenated in PEM format) and `ssl_certificate_key` (the private key in PEM format). For Let's Encrypt, use `fullchain.pem` as the certificate and `privkey.pem` as the key. Multiple certificate pairs can be specified (since nginx 1.11.0) for dual RSA+ECDSA setups — nginx automatically selects the right one based on the client's cipher preference. The `listen` directive must include `ssl`: `listen 443 ssl`.

sections.title: Protocol versions and cipher suites
sections.body: Set `ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3` — never enable SSLv3, TLSv1.0, or TLSv1.1. The default cipher string `HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5` is reasonable. For TLS 1.3, `ssl_prefer_server_ciphers off` is recommended since TLS 1.3 ciphers are all strong and client preference is fine. Check available ciphers with `openssl ciphers`. For maximum compatibility with older clients, keep TLSv1.2; for maximum security, use TLSv1.3 only.

sections.title: Session caching and performance
sections.body: SSL handshakes are CPU-intensive. Enable session caching to allow clients to resume sessions: `ssl_session_cache shared:SSL:10m` creates a 10MB shared cache across all workers (~40,000 sessions). Set `ssl_session_timeout 10m` for reuse duration. Enable `ssl_session_tickets on` for TLS session ticket resumption (RFC 5077). For reduced time-to-first-byte, set `ssl_buffer_size 4k` (default 16k sends larger TLS records).

sections.title: OCSP stapling
sections.body: OCSP stapling attaches the certificate's revocation status to the TLS handshake, eliminating the client's need to contact the CA. Enable with `ssl_stapling on` and `ssl_stapling_verify on`. Requires `ssl_trusted_certificate` pointing to the intermediate + root CA chain and a `resolver` directive for DNS lookups to the OCSP responder. Let's Encrypt's `chain.pem` works as the trusted certificate. Verify with: `openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -status`.

sections.title: Let's Encrypt with certbot
sections.body: Certbot automates certificate provisioning from Let's Encrypt. Install with `apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx`. Run `certbot --nginx -d example.com` to obtain a certificate and auto-configure nginx. Certbot modifies the server block to add SSL directives and creates a renewal timer. Certificates are stored in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/domain/`. Renewal is automatic via `certbot renew` (systemd timer). Rate limits: 50 certs per domain per week, 5 duplicate certs per week. Use `--staging` for testing to avoid rate limits.

sections.title: HTTP to HTTPS redirect
sections.body: Always redirect HTTP to HTTPS with a separate server block: `server { listen 80; server_name example.com; return 301 https://$host$request_uri; }`. Use `301` (permanent) not `302` (temporary) for SEO. Add HSTS header (`Strict-Transport-Security`) on the HTTPS server block to tell browsers to always use HTTPS. Include `preload` in HSTS if submitting to the browser preload list. The redirect server block must allow the `/.well-known/acme-challenge/` path for certbot HTTP-01 validation.

sections.title: Security headers
sections.body: Add security headers alongside SSL configuration: `Strict-Transport-Security` (HSTS, max-age=31536000), `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options: DENY` (or SAMEORIGIN), `X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block`, `Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin`, and `Content-Security-Policy`. Use `always` parameter so headers are sent even on error responses. These headers protect against clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and XSS attacks.
